label: Piacere: saying what you like
description: The liking construction, where the thing you like is the grammatical subject and the person doing the liking is an indirect object: mi piace la pizza is literally 'the pizza is pleasing to me'. The single biggest structural flip for English speakers at A1.

label: Building piacere sentences
description: Producing the pieces: the verb agreeing with the liked thing, the experiencer as clitic or a-phrase, the infinitive pattern, and the past with essere.

label: Piace or piacciono? Agreeing with the liked thing
description: The verb agrees with what is liked, not with who likes it: mi piace la pizza (singular) but mi piacciono gli spaghetti (plural).

attributes.common_miss: 'Mi piace i libri' and 'mi piacciono la pizza': agreement copied from the experiencer or just ignored.

label: Piace + infinitive
description: When what you like is an activity, the verb is always singular piace followed by the infinitive, even if the infinitive has a plural object: mi piace leggere i libri, never piacciono leggere.

attributes.common_miss: 'Mi piacciono leggere i gialli': the plural bait after the infinitive.

label: Who likes it: mi, ti, gli, le, Le, ci, vi
description: The person doing the liking is an indirect object clitic before the verb: mi, ti, gli (to him / to them), le (to her), Le (formal you), ci, vi.

attributes.common_miss: gli/le swapped, or a subject pronoun where the clitic belongs ('io piace').

label: A Marco piace: the a-phrase experiencer
description: Named or contrasted experiencers use a + noun or stressed pronoun instead of the clitic: A Marco piace il calcio. A me piace, a lei no. Using both at once (a me mi piace) is common in speech but redundant in standard Italian.

attributes.common_miss: Dropping the a ('Marco piace il calcio', which actually means people like Marco), and the redundant 'a me mi piace'.
attributes.authoring_note: Suggested marker treatment for 'a me mi piace': graded any_phrases entry at credit 0.5 with a register note, not a flat miss. Architect ruling recorded in the dispatch.
canonical_examples: A mia madre non piacciono i gatti.
A me piace, a lei no.

label: Past: mi è piaciuto, mi sono piaciuti
description: In the passato prossimo piacere takes essere, and the participle agrees with the liked thing: il film mi è piaciuto, le scarpe non mi sono piaciute. Two agreement decisions in one small sentence.

attributes.common_miss: avere as auxiliary ('mi ha piaciuto'), and the participle left masculine singular regardless of the liked thing.
canonical_examples: Il film mi è piaciuto molto.
Le scarpe non mi sono piaciute.

label: Using piacere well
description: The traps around the construction: the English subject flip, liking people, agreeing and disagreeing with someone's likes, the dispiacere false friend, and the wider family of verbs that behave the same way.

label: The 'I like' trap
description: Mapping English 'I like X' word-for-word produces io piaccio X, which is wrong (and piaccio actually means 'I am likeable / people like me'). The liked thing is the subject; the liker is an indirect object. This is the diagnostic heart of the topic.

attributes.common_miss: 'Io piaccio la pizza', 'io piace', 'piaccio la pizza': the subject flip in every variant.
canonical_examples: Mi piace il mare. (not 'Io piaccio il mare')

label: Mi piaci: liking people
description: When the liked thing is a person, the verb conjugates for that person: mi piaci (I like you), ti piaccio? (do you like me?), le piaci (she likes you). Recognition of these forms is a distinct skill from the mi piace pattern.

attributes.common_miss: Reading 'mi piaci' as 'you like me' (it is the reverse), and producing 'ti piaccio' when 'mi piaci' is meant.

label: Anche a me, neanche a me
description: Agreeing or disagreeing with someone's likes keeps the a-phrase: Mi piace il gelato. Anche a me (not anch'io). Non mi piace. Neanche a me. Contrast uses a me sì / a me no.

attributes.common_miss: 'Anch'io' in reply to 'mi piace...': the subject-pronoun reply English invites.
canonical_examples: Mi piace il gelato. Anche a me!
Non mi piacciono i cavoli. Neanche a me.
A me sì, a lui no.

label: Dispiacere is not 'to dislike'
description: Mi dispiace means 'I'm sorry', not 'I dislike it'. Dislike is non mi piace. And non mi dispiace is understatement for mild liking ('I don't mind it / I quite like it'). Ti dispiace + infinitive is a polite request.

attributes.common_miss: 'Mi dispiace il pesce' for 'I dislike fish', and reading 'non mi dispiace' as indifference rather than mild approval.

label: Verbs that work like piacere
description: The same experiencer-flip construction runs through mancare (mi manchi: I miss you), servire (mi servono due uova), bastare, interessare, sembrare, restare. Once piacere clicks, this family comes almost free, and mancare's direction flip (mi manchi = I miss you, not you miss me) is the sharpest test of whether it really has.

attributes.common_miss: 'Ti manco' produced for 'I miss you': the direction flip failing on mancare even when piacere is secure.
